chapters.body: ===Hypermodernism or; a history in how one slacker became imperial magistrate=== So the fate of their entire race had already been decided through a game of cards at an underwater whorehouse. [[carmen-de-caramel|Carmen, Archprovincess of the Joyanti Highlands]], had risen by the combined miracles of sea-to-land evolution, internet-learned flirtation, and her own reliable good looks, through the ranks of the [[Provisional Ruujoyantè|provisional government]] interregnum, while the dreaming god [[Orussorine|Orussorine]] waited to emerge from Her cocoon aboard that dopethrone called the [[Rifftree|Rifftree]]. And when asked what she intended to do about the [[Joyanti|Joyanti]] [[Joyanti climate crisis|catastrophe]], Carmen had only this to say: fucking-forget-about-it. The Joyanti don’t want to be saved, and I don’t want to save them either. Never mind who she had been before: some gutterwhore urchinette with scraped knees, hellbent on resuscitating her propagandized Joyanti-ism, whatever that means, through a series of dalliances with dames decidedly older—girls with chain-smoking fortitudes, kush-coughs, and that bored, brave approach to womanhood which expresses itself through plant adoption, casual sex, and the purchase of increasingly musk-scented candles. Nonetheless, there she was, ruler of the [[Joyanti Highlands|Highlands of Ruujoyanté]], her nation-state, which was sinking into the [[Diamond Sea|Diamond Sea]] at a rate of six feet per month. She had no business being on dry land. Really, none. She was just like all the rest of them: born underwater in [[Sunken Adore|Sunken Adore]], bred up toward abyssal manners, to curtsey in currents, to wait, always, for Her to bite back. The shallows of the Diamond Sea, and that resident city under-the-dome with its mother-of-pearl municipal rot, cared for nobody. Carmen’s political life was born of altruistic sociopathy. It began like this. Carmen had been really craving egg-rolls. One of the ways hypermodern life announces itself is through the sheer variety of food from every paradise, province, dump, peninsula, refugee kitchenette, and rain-slicked shithole on this spinning rock called E____. Every race has its own cuisine, and every race, with touching provincial idiocy, believes theirs to be the best; but what Carmen could tell you after a night out drinking with other girls from her rotating friend-group of rebellious literature students, meth-addled crustpunkettes and their polycule of equally hirsutian harlots, restaurant workers kept on a glittery leash by their cocaine dealers, hand-on-chin and slouching around in thrift-store vintageria, chic embroidered summer dresses, all pink-and-brown, camo-print, leopardfur simulacra, mascara in every minor key of brown, plus the artisanal candle she had purchased earlier that day while drunk on Aperol and the warm, syrupy suspicion that her friend was coming onto her, and drunker still on her own private method of divine witchcraft, which she hoped, blush-bless it, might actually work—was this: the future lay somewhere up north, not here, not in her underwater city, not in the seadome’s damp aquarium of inherited little dooms. What? Didn’t you know this? Neoliberalism had its heyday back when the world still mistook sincerity for a failure to mythologize irony, and Carmen, taking inventory of herself through the smeared compact-mirror of history, had begun to suspect that although meth tasted like burnt plastic, it feels really, really good when she touches me there and hey! I think she really likes me now, maybe it’s true what they say, maybe there is a God! A few years of this, of studying the study-sessions the hairy art hoes underwent in their little harems of theory, ashtrays, wet boots, stolen perfumes, unpaid rent, and devotional self-abasement, made Carmen reconsider it all: her own life, her upbringing, and whether the superficial boutique of fashionable poverty in her home city of Sunken Adore was really all there was. She’d need a few more drinks to decide, so pour her some and let’s continue. “Duuuuuuuude. Wanna get something over at Miley’s? I’ve been dying for their maple-glazed donut-chicken-tender-supreme, whatever-the-fuck-it’s-called.” Carmen says this between bong hits on April 2nd, 3007, which is to say in the petite and epochal hour when history, smelling of burnt green and girlshampoo, forgets to keep its knees together. The hovel of the harem has reached a new domestic nadir: pizza boxes fucking everywhere, underwear promoted to lampshade, a whole taxonomy of cups half-full of something amber, hostile, medicinal, and the girls moonlighting through impromptu speed-sessions in which it is always 3 a.m. somewhere. One of the purveyors of drugs is face-first before the CRT, her pretty blue eyes receiving, a half-second late, the gunfire and grand theft auto she has been spiritually subcontracted to perform; so when she turns that delayed deadpan stare upon Carmen who feels a little excited trying to guess whether the tiny criminal figures are still running around inside those irises. “If we can get takeout,” the girl says, “I’m kinda busy.” “Don’t be a lazy fuck.” This from the vodka-sommelier and mistress de la maison, who has just slammed the freezer shut then waltzed in socks through the sad confetti of yesterday’s pleasures over to stand in front of the Entertainment, thereby eclipsing its blue violence with her own hungover radiance. “Need to get out of the house. We’ll meet Ivy and the others there too.” Where is Miss de no Marrez —N23097, starstruck angel of the highlands?" "Just a moment ago she was over by the pomcurry stalls—" "Of course she was, that demongirl!" ===Nevina of the Joyanti Highlands=== It was in the fizz and flutter of such gossip that the immense Caravan 1099 Sugarcube made her progress, orange-steel frame stalwart against the dense, turtle-teal immensities of the Diamond Sea. Within her rode some four hundred and thirty-nine thousand, six hundred and fifty-two Joyanti girls — once citizenettes of the Ruujoyanté nation, which, at this moment, year 3009, was engaged in a slow descent of six feet per day towards the seabed. The harem had only just awakened into being, each girl smelling of bubblegum anaesthesia, their bodies cozy in state-issued cartoon pajamas whose sanctioned whimsy had not yet been outgrown. Local religious leaders mothered them into a wavering single-file just beyond the grand hall: priestesses of Her Ideal & Splendor, perpetually fidgeting, drunk on dreams, once attendants of Seminary with a decorous seriousness in their Chosen Theology, lately decanted from that pedagogical nest and made to hover a few inches taller on the tips of their toes above the girls of their private cult, failing still to finalize which iteration of the God-Empress to adore — She of the Many Colors. Each of them, some softly incalculable tally of countesses, missionaries, devotional interns rummaging in the tasteful ruins of the Sunken Race, professes to know very little of Her nature, and yet their hands betray them: those ever-frail, ever-slender, conspicuously clean and ruinously soft appendages, alongside the velveted luxury of their boredom (less absence than disguise, a satin mask stretched taut over a rather operatic insecurity), the way they shuffle, arrange, collapse into seats, exhausted — these minute choreographies permitting the watching girls to wonder, to dream, to hope in ways no catechism could quite authorize, knowledge accruing damp and lived, a slow bodily annotation. Once the Caravan crossed into international waters, the pupae began to dream in little lacquered cocoons where long plastic tubes offered a sweet, confectionary vapor, a menu of inhalable pleasures from which each selected her own signature bouquet. So they drifted inward, into weather-systems of their private minds, climates entirely their own, complete with storms, clearings, and occasional miraculous forecasts. The journey lasted four months, during which roughly half the girls traveled in this narcotized elegance — it was considered a luxury to be permitted sleep at all, let alone the gentle indulgence of IV drips calibrated to one's preferred soda-pop of the day, or trays of synthetic onigiri, and, for those whose dreams proved suitable for show-and-tell, the rare presentation of choco-cocobuns. Not all of them, it should be said, would be permitted warm water. Nevina de no Marrez, sixteen, had been rehearsing this moment all her life. She keeps it tucked into the memory of her shampooed hair, her mother Nora’s voice lingering there: "remember, Vina," she says, massaging in coconut-aloe, Muulu is what you make of it, go anywhere, yes, fate may already have penciled you in somewhere, but don't you dare let the city press you flat — be free, keep that personality shamelessly on your sleeve." Years at Lastragotha Mire Academy had nearly persuaded Nora into the silhouette of a warm, attentive woman, if not quite mother then something adjacent and trying, and Vina, adrift now, consoles herself with the smallness of the body: she has not sweated since before her last bath, and so refuses, even in thought, to rinse from her braided brown hair that last soft maternal fragrance. She sleeps, eventually, as they all do — reclined upon the High Seas, each girl sealed into her own small climate. Ruujoyanté, by 3009, is a beautifully mismanaged catastrophe — an ecological fever dream with good lighting. The Joyanti had long declared themselves keepers of land that would not keep, of a peninsular-archipelagic darling-state, their traits tuned across generations to the minute whims of that locale, sentenced in effect to a maritime fate until, in a flourish of late-stage ingenuity (or perhaps hysteria dressed as progress), the conglomerate-faiths, ARCON foremost among them, paired devotion with research and a kind of starved brilliance: the hyperanthro programs, those anorexic devotees of the Muulan Academy of the Starstruck, discovering clandestine currents of astrological ambition. So — a reprieve of sorts. Girls might now recline, sip cherrycoke, be delivered into dreams exceeding even their own well-trained capacities for wonder. They were, so the phrase went, bound to the cervix. ===The Archprovincesses=== Muulu had already reveived seventeen caravans, each larger than the last. This one was the Third of the Great Ones, and once the directives filtered down from the Archprovincess back in Sunken Adore, her own jealousy seemed finally to be at its wit’s end. “It’s obscene!” She declared, pacing in a slouching pull-over in the confines of her chamberoom. She had told her harem that she was to stop drinking but that had lasted a day and the weather in the deep sea permits one to stave away the dark, so she’s now adorned in sleepwear adjusting the filtered seawater for her absinthe machine, eyes tired and mauve. “That we allow, encourage even, the lowliest of the nation to be shipped first class to the Maternal City, while I, diplomacy incarnate, haven’t even been granted a polite visit.” A servant-girl, brave or doomed, offered softly: “Archprovincess Mahala, the Book of Tehom 31:9 — “the wholeness of spirit comes from the purity of action; let one’s hands remain dirty from the sins of others.” Mahala was not amused. Coughing as she drank the absinthe, she flopped backwards on her plush-laden bed and sighed, sliding down. Her burden of duty did not exhaust her (only a little), moreso the humiliation of not being able to recite scripture on command. Her pink skippers waited exiled across the room; She of the Perpetual Fatigue, remembered the duty of others and snapped at them to place the slippers on her feet. Kneeling low, the servant asked: "Shall I fetch an aspirin?" The Archprovincess of the Highlands, Mahala, had lately liberated the de no Marrez Family along with a tasteful handful of communal agriculturists from the Faresian north, and what did she have to show for it? "A thank-you would be nice," she remarked, examining her reflection, "a modest statue, perhaps, a roaming jihad or two." Instead, she and the remaining regional powers convened in perfumed chambers, exchanging secrets and the occasional kiss to stave off civil war over access to the one true Heaven, eventually settling on the long, beige fatigue of decentralization at which point ARCON arrived, dispatching private armies to the peninsula with a smile: full access to the southern, sinking islands, please and thank you. Fine, just don't fuck it all up — there are deep-sea secrets we prefer undisturbed, and the Orangii tribes are to remain tastefully untouched, culturally preserved, you understand. What followed, inevitably, was the birth of a new metagod: neocolonial feudalism in a well-tailored suit, under whose patronage Sunken Adore began to thrive in its aqueous afterlife, and a new race of girls emerged — one that did not approve of Mahala, her manners too starched, her doctrine too corseted, all that antique gravitas worn like an ill-fitting tiara. "What they don't understand," she is fond of saying, a fingertip tapping the air, "is that a life without structure dissolves — poof, gone. Let them play at freedom; they'll come crawling back to form." The household, meanwhile, had caught its own velvet fever: the servant-girls, prickled by the sight of unwashed masses drifting off toward imagined futures, folded themselves more tightly around their mistress, clustering at her feet in a synchronized shimmer of identical haircuts and uniforms. At dinner they scurried, then arranged themselves along the dim perimeter of the room in coral and marine regalia evoking the mystique of a Victorian absinthe parlor. "Well?" Mahala sighed, languid, delighted by her own fatigue, "am I to dine alone or be admired first?" They hoped, of course, to be chosen — sent outward as emissaries of her image, and she indulged the fantasy, having already secured a few such opportunities over an underwater card table in the Red Light District. She had quarreled with more than a few sinful girls of the sinking nation there, always with that polished hauteur of a theologically learn’d lass from high places. She had gained her post, Archprovincess of the Highlands, having once been a ladder-climbing creature out of the slums of Sunken Adore, and what had followed was a sequence of nights best described as instructive excess: ultramandarine haze, a star wished upon beyond the shallows, and then, in a pivot both elegant and appalling, a hit so clean it caused seasoned mercenaries from here to Muulu to lift their brows in professional admiration. Her target: a nomadic oceanist-imperial hybrid cultist of the Maternal City, rumored to be practically proprietary to her mother-mistress in Her inner court. Doors opened and she found herself at the famed diplomatic poker nights, dive-bar liturgies where the lowest of the low rehearse power with cheap chips and expensive grudges, each of them debating who had been the better martyr for the Joyanti cause. “Martyrdom is everything,” someone quips, shuffling; “baby, curate it.” They argue over cynar negronis about who best suffered: Worry, the synthpop-prophetess presently evolving in Muulu, her physiognomy adapting to dancing with the divine; one of her innumerable exes with a dungeon-synth side project and a tragic relationship to the sun; the solarpunk pixie dreamgirl who retired into a vegan co-op in Redleaf, coasting off the fame of having come closest to Worry’s hand in marriage before fumbling it across a string of podcasts—declaring all meat murder, recommending public executions for flesh-eaters, and in the process seeding a whole minor industry of rumors about their past sex lives, assuming this dogma. Through it all, the Archprovincess of the Hyperlight Highway ensconced in black-gold full-body replacement, legs crossed with a sleekness bordering on sin, waves off Worry with characteristic economy: “Martyr? Please. A traitor whore to the Joyanti bloodline.” Another aristocrat, the Archprovincess of the Orangiian Archipelago, lingers in appraisal, admiring the way her caramel skin refracts itself in the other’s sleek obsidian synthetics, before slipping her voice in: “Who, then—ARCON? Haven’t you sold enough of your soul already? Do leave the rest of us with something unauctioned.” A flick of the wrist, she folds. “The real martyrs are the diver girls—those poor girls who die for the Caravans. It wasn’t enough to make the vessels enormous; now they fabricate girls to ride them. What culture remains in Ruujoyanté, if not a rehearsal for submission to imperial appetite?” In the corner, Mahala drew slowly from the gilded hookah, lips glossed in smoke, watching with a studied, almost decorative disgust. Yes, the Caravans had been a blight, everyone knew this, conditioning girls to abandon their lives, to re-emerge as evangelists of a newly arranged hyperrace…and yet, she wondered, what was to be done for the millions presently drowning by the day? She remained one of the few still seduced by the idea of sending them off toward something better, with the vague promise they might return once the damage was mended, once the cause was properly named. "Extreme lunar tides," someone offered. "Cosmic restructuring," another. "Karma," a third. Soon after, the whores had arrived and the four or five seated at the private table had all been instructed to not look upon their bare breasts and neat bodies. Of course, this was the game in oriental cafes, one gets off on not knowing, only sensing. Their eyes soon shifted and moved towards a HUD of the House’s design. Lights dimmed and soon enough there would be a display of their cards projected on their corneas and the game would resume there. Only voices could be distinguished. One of the few girls Mahala found herself quietly aligned with, an emissary, no less, to the new sapling where their living gods were rumored to germinate, sat just across from her in a spill of golden lightengaged beneath the table in a private, playful duel of footsie that intensified whenever the talk grew drearily administrative. "So 1099's more or less done," she began, twirling a stemmed glass so the amber caught and fractured into honeyed shards, "but a few of ARCON's shadow builders sent an evangelist up to the capital with complaints: water temperature too high, whale-brain network not syncing with the girls back home — and something about food shortages, and the possibility of 1099 being something like 1098 or 1097..." "Impossible." The Archprovincess of the Hyperlight Highway waved her hand again.The metal that ran up the sides of her neck, now affixed with RealFlesh, had begun to glow as if to blush in anger. "It's always a catastrophe with them. What are we paying for — delays, excuses, theatrical incompetence? The ocean's too hot now? As if they aren't setting it ablaze themselves building the fucking things." “That’s true,” Mahala offered, tone velveted into neutrality, though her foot pressed just a fraction firmer beneath the table. “We’ve had record beachings—whales, entire skeins of sealife. Isn’t that meant to fall under the Orangii’s jurisdiction?” That girl, whho had been delicately dosing sugar cubes with drops of LSD, was being handfed the wondercandies by one of the veiled whores as she sat on her lapeyes caught in the refracted prisms of cynar swirling through her crystal highball. She leaned back. “Our population is double yours with a third of the space. Anytime, Mahala, you feel like a vacation down on the islands, do come—do help us tidy up the little genocides that keep happening when the food runs out.” "So send them aboard the ships," Mahala said. "Lost cause, yadda yadda yadda." The emissary to the Rifftree—eyes that peculiar mauve that always pulled from Mahala a memory of lavender bodywash tilted her head, lips glossed, speaking in that familiar lispy cadence === Caravaneers=== ===The Grand Deposition=== ===The Nude Penitencer===
